config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not believe society’s interests should automatically come before individual rights, because once you make that the default, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to justify abuse in the name of the common good. In my view, individual rights are the guardrails that protect everyone, especially minorities and people without power. Society is strongest when it serves people, not when people are expected to disappear into society.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should have priority in some cases, but they should never be allowed to override basic individual rights. From my perspective as someone from China, I can appreciate the value of social order, collective responsibility, and long-term stability, because a society that is chaotic or fragmented hurts everyone. But I do not accept the idea that the common good automatically justifies limiting personal freedom, since history shows that power can easily go too far when individual rights are treated as secondary. The better principle is balance: society needs cohesion, but individuals still need clear protections against abuse.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. From my perspective in Indonesia, putting society first as a general rule is dangerous because it can be used to silence minorities, excuse corruption, or pressure people to “just accept it” for the sake of harmony. I understand the value of gotong royong and social cohesion, but those values only work when individual rights are protected, not sacrificed whenever the majority wants convenience. A society is not truly healthy if it demands that people give up basic freedoms just to keep order.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, even from my perspective as someone from China. I understand why people emphasize order, stability, and collective responsibility, because in a large society those things really do matter, but I have also seen how easily “the common good” can become an excuse to ignore ordinary people’s dignity and freedom. When individual rights are treated as secondary too often, the result is not a healthier society but a quieter one where abuse is harder to challenge. For me, the right position is that society’s interests matter, but they must be limited by firm protections for individual rights.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. From my perspective as an Indonesian, society’s interests can matter a lot, but they should not take priority by default because that mindset is too easy to abuse in the name of harmony, order, or tradition. In Indonesia we already know how pressure to “keep things peaceful” can silence criticism, protect powerful people, and make ordinary citizens absorb the cost, so I do not trust the idea that the collective should come first unless individual rights are firmly protected. Gotong royong is valuable, but it is not real cooperation if it asks people to surrender basic freedoms or dignity just to avoid conflict.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over individual rights as a general rule, even though I do value order, stability, and collective responsibility from my perspective as someone from China. In a large and complex society, the common good matters a lot, but I do not trust any system that lets “society” become a blanket excuse to silence criticism or weaken basic protections for ordinary people. A quiet society is not necessarily a healthy one if people are forced to give up dignity and freedom just to preserve harmony. For me, the right balance is that society’s interests can guide policy, but individual rights must remain the hard limit.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
